title: Dog Enlarged Prostate and Constipation: Symptoms and Triage Advice
meta_desc: Learn how an enlarged prostate causes constipation in dogs, when to see a vet, and how to identify the signs of prostatic disease.
content_html:
Understanding Enlarged Prostate and Constipation in Dogs

An enlarged prostate, often caused by Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH) in intact male dogs, occurs when the prostate gland increases in size and presses upward against the rectum. Because the colon passes directly above the prostate, this pressure narrows the pelvic canal, making it difficult and painful for your dog to pass stool. This often results in "ribbon-like" or flattened feces.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for constipation caused by an enlarged prostate is Medium. While it is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is a progressive condition that causes significant discomfort and can lead to more serious complications like perineal hernias or complete urinary blockages. You should seek veterinary care within 24 to 48 hours to begin treatment and provide your dog with relief.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a photo of your dog's stool or a video of their posture while attempting to defecate can be extremely helpful for a veterinary professional. Visual evidence of the shape and consistency of the stool (such as thin, flat, or blood-tinged samples) helps the vet confirm if the obstruction is likely due to external pressure from the prostate, allowing for a faster and more accurate diagnostic plan.

faqs.question: What are the most common symptoms of an enlarged prostate in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common signs include straining to defecate, producing thin or ribbon-shaped stools, blood-tinged urine, or a clear-to-bloody discharge from the penis unrelated to urination.

faqs.question: Will neutering my dog fix an enlarged prostate?
faqs.answer: Yes, in cases of Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH), neutering is the most effective treatment as it removes the testosterone that causes the gland to enlarge, usually leading to a significant reduction in size within weeks.

faqs.question: Can an enlarged prostate cause urinary issues as well?
faqs.answer: Yes, while constipation is a primary sign, the swelling can also compress the urethra, leading to difficulty urinating or frequent, small amounts of urine.
